name: Adhirasam
description: Adhirasam is a traditional deep-fried South Indian sweet made from raw rice flour and jaggery syrup, flavoured with cardamom and dry ginger. It has a unique texture — crispy on the edges and pillowy soft inside. A Diwali essential in Tamil Nadu, making perfect adhirasam requires patience and practice, especially in getting the jaggery syrup consistency and rice flour moisture right. The dough is rested for a day before frying, which develops its characteristic flavour and texture.

steps.instruction: Wash raw rice and soak in water for 3 hours.
Drain and spread the rice on a clean white cotton cloth for 30 minutes. The rice should still have moisture — when pressed, a few grains should stick to your hand. Do not let it dry completely.
steps.tip: Completely dry rice gives rubbery adhirasam.

steps.instruction: Grind the rice in a mixer in 2 batches to a semi-fine powder. It should be slightly coarse, not as fine as store-bought flour.
Sieve the flour. Return coarse remains to the mixer and grind again with the next batch.
steps.tip: Do not use a very fine sieve — slightly bigger holes give the right coarse flour texture.

steps.instruction: Add sesame seeds, dry ginger powder and cardamom powder to the flour. Mix well, cover with a moist cloth and set aside.
Crush jaggery, add ½ cup water and heat until dissolved. Strain to remove impurities.
Heat the strained jaggery syrup again on medium flame, stirring occasionally. Keep checking consistency by pouring drops into a bowl of water.

At the correct stage, the syrup will not dissolve in water. You will be able to gather it with your fingers and form a loose, jelly-like soft ball that slides easily from your hand. This is soft ball consistency — switch off immediately.
steps.tip: Jaggery syrup consistency is the most critical step. Do not let it pass soft ball stage.

steps.instruction: Add the rice flour to the hot syrup little by little, mixing continuously without lumps. The dough will be slightly loose.
Transfer to an airtight container and rest at room temperature for 1 day. The dough will thicken and become firm.
steps.tip: Resting the dough for at least 1 day is mandatory — it ferments slightly and develops flavour.

steps.instruction: The next day, add 1 tsp ghee and knead the dough once. Pinch and roll into lemon-sized balls.
steps.tip: If dough is too tight, sprinkle a little hot water and knead. If too loose, add 1 to 2 tbsp idiyappam flour.

steps.instruction: Grease a banana leaf or zip-lock cover with ghee. Pat each ball into a slightly thick round disc.
Heat oil on low-medium heat. Slide the patted adhirasam into the oil.
steps.tip: Fry on low-medium heat so the inside cooks through while the outside turns crisp.

steps.instruction: Let it rise up, then pour hot oil over it so it puffs up. Flip and pour oil again.
Fry until deep red in colour. Use two ladles to gently squeeze out excess oil.
Drain on 2 to 3 layers of kitchen tissues. Cool and store.
steps.tip: Adhirasam stays good for 2 to 3 days at room temperature. For longer storage, refrigerate and steam lightly before serving.

tips: Choosing the correct raw rice (maavu pacharisi) and jaggery (paagu vellam) is the first key to authentic adhirasam.
The rice must have some moisture after drying — completely dry rice gives rubbery adhirasam.
Do not use a very fine sieve — slightly bigger holes give the right coarse flour texture.
Jaggery syrup consistency is the most critical step. Do not let it pass soft ball stage.
Resting the dough for at least 1 day is mandatory — it ferments slightly and develops flavour.
Fry on low-medium heat so the inside cooks through while the outside turns crisp.
If dough is too tight, sprinkle a little hot water and knead. If too loose, add 1 to 2 tbsp idiyappam flour.
Adhirasam stays good for 2 to 3 days at room temperature. For longer storage, refrigerate and steam lightly before serving.
